subject: Computer Networking
title: Packet Switching Invented
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Packet Switching Invented.

hook.text: In 1961, Leonard Kleinrock proposed splitting data into small blocks that could each find their own way.

buildup.text: Circuit switching wasted bandwidth — the full line was reserved even during silence. Packets fixed that.

discovery.text: Paul Baran at RAND and Donald Davies at NPL independently developed packet switching in the early 1960s.

twist.text: The U.S. military funded Baran's work. They needed a network that could survive nuclear attack by rerouting packets.

climax.text: Packet switching became the foundation of ARPANET and eventually the entire internet.

punchline.text: One idea — split data into packets — created the modern internet.

quiz.question: Why was packet switching more efficient than circuit switching?
options: Bandwidth wasn't wasted on idle connections
